original_abstract:
Ferroelectric (FE) semiconductors like $\text{BaTiO}_3$ possess a remnant polarization that can be leveraged to drive the separation of photogenerated charge carriers. In this work, we provide the first demonstration of FE-enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution and photoelectrochemical water oxidation using barium titanate nanocrystals. Single-crystalline nanocrystals with a ferroelectric tetragonal structure were synthesized via a hydrothermal route from $\text{TiO}_2$ and barium hydroxide with a 63% yield.

When deposited on tantalum substrates, these $\text{BaTiO}_3$ nanocrystal films achieve water oxidation photocurrents of $0.141 \text{ mA cm}^{-2}$ at $1.23 \text{ V}_{\text{RHE}}$ under UV illumination ($60 \text{ mW cm}^{-2}$). By applying an electric polarization of $52.8 \text{ kV cm}^{-1}$ normal to the film plane, we could modulate the performance: the photocurrent increased by a factor of 2 or decreased by a factor of 3.5, depending on the field polarity. This polarization also induced shifts in the onset potential by $-0.15 \text{ V}$ or $+0.09 \text{ V}$ and significantly modified the surface photovoltage (SPV) response. 

Furthermore, pre-exposure to an electric field enhanced the photocatalytic $\text{H}_2$ evolution rate of $\text{Pt/BaTiO}_3$ by approximately 1.5 times and improved the facet-selective photodeposition of silver onto the (001) nanocrystal surfaces. Crucially, all FE-induced enhancements were reversible and could be neutralized by heating the samples above the Curie temperature of $\text{BaTiO}_3$. These phenomena be attributed to FE dipole-induced modifications of the potential drop across the material's space charge layer. These results highlight the potential of using the ferroelectric effect to design more efficient solar-to-fuel conversion systems.
humanized_abstract:
Ferroelectric (FE) semiconductors such as $\text{BaTiO}_3$ have a permanent remnant polarization that can be utilized to separate photogenerated charges. To the best of our knowledge, this work represents the first example of utilizing FE-enhanced ferroelectric semiconductor for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution (HY) and photoelectrochemical (PEC) water oxidation. Single crystalline barium titanate (BaTiO3, BTO) ferroelectric nanocrystals were successfully synthesized through hydrothermal reaction from $\text{TiO}_2$ and $\text{Ba(OH)}_2$ precursors with 63% yield.

Water oxidation photocurrents of 0.141 mA cm−2 at 1.23 VRHE were achieved under UV illumination (60 mW cm−2) for $\text{BaTiO}_3$ nanocrystal films grown on tantalum. Applying an electric polarization of 52.8 kV cm−1 along the normal direction to the film surface can enhance or diminish the photocurrent by a factor of 2 or up to 3.5 depending on the applied field polarity. The onset potential for the photocurrent was also shifted by −0.15 V or +0.09 V, while the surface photovoltage (SPV) signal was dramatically changed.

Enhanced solar-driven hydrogen evolution from water using metastably grown BaTiO3 nanoparticles through ferroelectric-field pre-exposure Photoanode material architecture, which leads to high solar-to-hydrogen (STH) efficiency for water-splitting systems, is of significant interest. We present an unprecedented approach by demonstrating that metastably grown BaTiO3 nanoparticles could be pre-exposed to an electric field that enhances the solar driven hydrogen evolution activity (photocatalytic H2 evolution) of Pt/BaTiO3 material by ~1.5 times and the facet-selective photodeposition of silver on the (001) surface of nanocrystals. Most importantly, the whole FE-induced enhancements were reversible and could be eliminated when samples are heated above the Curie temperature of BaTiO3. We believe that these FE-induced dipole-effects are likely due to the alteration of the space charge layer potential drop. Ultimately, the strategy using ferroelectric effect to enhance solar-driven hydrogen evolution from water is demonstrated.

Most importantly, the whole FE-induced enhancements were reversible and could be eliminated when samples are heated above the Curie temperature of BaTiO3. Ultimately, the strategy using ferroelectric effect to enhance solar-driven hydrogen evolution from water is demonstrated.
document.input:
Ferroelectric (FE) semiconductors like $\text{BaTiO}_3$ possess a remnant polarization that can be leveraged to drive the separation of photogenerated charge carriers. In this work, we provide the first demonstration of FE-enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution and photoelectrochemical water oxidation using barium titanate nanocrystals. Furthermore, pre-exposure to an electric field enhanced the photocatalytic $\text{H}_2$ evolution rate of $\text{Pt/BaTiO}_3$ by approximately 1.5 times and improved the facet-selective photodeposition of silver onto the (001) nanocrystal surfaces. Crucially, all FE-induced enhancements were reversible and could be neutralized by heating the samples above the Curie temperature of $\text{BaTiO}_3$. These phenomena be attributed to FE dipole-induced modifications of the potential drop across the material's space charge layer. These results highlight the potential of using the ferroelectric effect to design more efficient solar-to-fuel conversion systems.
